# 1o1-utils

> Lightweight, tree-shakeable TypeScript utility library. 2 kB gzipped, zero dependencies.

Install: npm install 1o1-utils
Import: import { chunk, pick, slugify, retry } from "1o1-utils";
Individual: import { chunk } from "1o1-utils/chunk";

All functions use named object parameters: fn({ param1, param2 }).

---

## Arrays

### arrayToHash

Convert an array of objects into a hash map keyed by a property. Useful for fast lookups by a unique identifier.

Import: import { arrayToHash } from "1o1-utils/array-to-hash";

Signature:
function arrayToHash<T>({ array, key }: ArrayToHashParams<T>): Record<string, T>

Parameters:
- array (T[], required): The array of objects to convert
- key (keyof T, required): The property to use as hash key

Returns: Record<string, T>

Example:
const users = [
  { id: "a1", name: "Alice" },
  { id: "b2", name: "Bob" },
];
arrayToHash({ array: users, key: "id" });
// => { a1: { id: "a1", name: "Alice" }, b2: { id: "b2", name: "Bob" } }

Throws: Error if array is not an array. Error if key is not a string.
Skips items where the key value is falsy or not a string.

---

### chunk

Split an array into smaller arrays of a specified size. The last chunk may contain fewer elements.

Import: import { chunk } from "1o1-utils/chunk";

Signature:
function chunk<T>({ array, size }: ChunkParams<T>): T[][]

Parameters:
- array (T[], required): The array to split
- size (number, required): Max items per chunk (positive integer)

Returns: T[][]

Example:
chunk({ array: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], size: 2 });
// => [[1, 2], [3, 4], [5]]

Throws: Error if array is not an array. Error if size is not a positive integer.
Returns [] for an empty array.

---

### groupBy

Group array elements into an object keyed by the value of a specified property.

Import: import { groupBy } from "1o1-utils/group-by";

Signature:
function groupBy<T>({ array, key }: GroupByParams<T>): Record<string, T[]>

Parameters:
- array (T[], required): The array to group
- key (keyof T, required): The property to group by

Returns: Record<string, T[]>

Example:
const items = [
  { name: "Alice", role: "admin" },
  { name: "Bob", role: "user" },
  { name: "Carol", role: "admin" },
];
groupBy({ array: items, key: "role" });
// => { admin: [{ name: "Alice", role: "admin" }, { name: "Carol", role: "admin" }], user: [{ name: "Bob", role: "user" }] }

Throws: Error if array is not an array. Error if key is undefined or null.
Groups are keyed by String(value[key]).

---

### sortBy

Return a new array sorted by a specified property. Supports ascending/descending and dot notation for nested properties.

Import: import { sortBy } from "1o1-utils/sort-by";

Signature:
function sortBy<T>({ array, key, order = "asc" }: SortByParams<T>): T[]

Parameters:
- array (T[], required): The array to sort
- key (keyof T | string, required): Property to sort by (supports dot notation)
- order ("asc" | "desc", optional): Sort direction (default: "asc")

Returns: T[] — A new sorted array (does not mutate the original).

Example:
sortBy({ array: [{ age: 30 }, { age: 20 }], key: "age" });
// => [{ age: 20 }, { age: 30 }]

sortBy({ array: [{ age: 30 }, { age: 20 }], key: "age", order: "desc" });
// => [{ age: 30 }, { age: 20 }]

sortBy({ array: users, key: "address.city" }); // nested dot notation

Throws: Error if array is not an array. Error if key is undefined or null.
Supports both numeric and string comparisons.

---

### unique

Return a new array with duplicate elements removed. For objects, specify a key to determine uniqueness.

Import: import { unique } from "1o1-utils/unique";

Signature:
function unique<T>({ array, key }: UniqueParams<T>): T[]

Parameters:
- array (T[], required): The array to deduplicate
- key (keyof T, optional): Property to determine uniqueness for objects

Returns: T[]

Example:
unique({ array: [1, 2, 2, 3, 1] });
// => [1, 2, 3]

unique({ array: [{ id: 1, name: "Alice" }, { id: 2, name: "Bob" }, { id: 1, name: "Alice Copy" }], key: "id" });
// => [{ id: 1, name: "Alice" }, { id: 2, name: "Bob" }]

Throws: Error if array is not an array.
Without key, uses Set for primitive deduplication. With key, keeps the first occurrence.

---

## Objects

### cloneDeep

Create a deep clone of any value. Handles plain objects, arrays, Date, RegExp, Map, Set, typed arrays, ArrayBuffer, Error, and circular references. Functions are copied by reference.

Import: import { cloneDeep } from "1o1-utils/clone-deep";

Signature:
function cloneDeep<T>({ value }: CloneDeepParams<T>): T

Parameters:
- value (T, required): The value to clone

Returns: T — A deep clone of the input. The return type matches the input type.

Example:
const original = { a: { b: [1, 2] }, date: new Date() };
const cloned = cloneDeep({ value: original });
cloned.a.b.push(3);
original.a.b; // [1, 2] — unaffected

Primitives are returned as-is. Functions are copied by reference. Map values are deep cloned but keys are kept by reference. Circular references are detected via a WeakMap. Uses an iterative, stack-based algorithm to avoid stack overflow on deeply nested structures. Custom class prototype chains are not preserved.

---

### deepMerge

Recursively merge two objects into a new object. Nested plain objects are merged deeply; arrays and other types are overwritten.

Import: import { deepMerge } from "1o1-utils/deep-merge";

Signature:
function deepMerge({ target, source }: DeepMergeParams): Record<string, unknown>

Parameters:
- target (Record<string, unknown>, required): The base object
- source (Record<string, unknown>, required): The object to merge into target

Returns: Record<string, unknown> — A new merged object (neither input is mutated).

Example:
deepMerge({
  target: { a: 1, b: { x: 10 } },
  source: { b: { y: 20 }, c: 3 },
});
// => { a: 1, b: { x: 10, y: 20 }, c: 3 }

Throws: Error if target is not a plain object. Error if source is not a plain object.
Uses a stack-based (non-recursive) algorithm to avoid stack overflow on deeply nested objects.

---

### defaults

Fill `undefined` (or missing) properties in target with values from source. Existing defined values — including `null`, `0`, `""`, and `false` — are preserved. Only shallow; for nested objects use `defaultsDeep`.

Import: import { defaults } from "1o1-utils/defaults";

Signature:
function defaults({ target, source }: DefaultsParams): Record<string, unknown>

Parameters:
- target (Record<string, unknown>, required): The base object whose defined values win
- source (Record<string, unknown>, required): The object supplying defaults for missing keys

Returns: Record<string, unknown> — A new object (neither input is mutated).

Example:
defaults({
  target: { a: 1 },
  source: { a: 99, b: 2 },
});
// => { a: 1, b: 2 }

defaults({
  target: { a: null, b: 0, c: "" },
  source: { a: 1, b: 99, c: "fallback", d: 4 },
});
// => { a: null, b: 0, c: "", d: 4 }

Throws: Error if target is not a plain object. Error if source is not a plain object.
Only `undefined` (or missing) keys are replaced — other falsy values are preserved.

---

### defaultsDeep

Recursively fill `undefined` (or missing) properties in target with values from source. When both sides have plain objects at the same key, they are merged recursively. Arrays in target are preserved (not merged with source arrays).

Import: import { defaultsDeep } from "1o1-utils/defaults-deep";

Signature:
function defaultsDeep({ target, source }: DefaultsDeepParams): Record<string, unknown>

Parameters:
- target (Record<string, unknown>, required): The base object whose defined values win
- source (Record<string, unknown>, required): The object supplying defaults for missing keys

Returns: Record<string, unknown> — A new object (neither input is mutated).

Example:
defaultsDeep({
  target: { db: { port: 5432 } },
  source: { db: { port: 3306, host: "localhost" }, debug: false },
});
// => { db: { port: 5432, host: "localhost" }, debug: false }

Throws: Error if target is not a plain object. Error if source is not a plain object.
Uses a stack-based (non-recursive) algorithm. Arrays and non-plain-object values in target are treated as leaves — not merged.

---

### get

Read a nested value from an object using a dot-notation path. Returns a default value (or undefined) when the path does not resolve. Never throws.

Import: import { get } from "1o1-utils/get";

Signature:
function get<T>({ obj, path, defaultValue }: GetParams<T>): unknown

Parameters:
- obj (T, required): The source object
- path (string, required): Dot-notation path (e.g. "address.city")
- defaultValue (unknown, optional): Returned when the path does not resolve

Returns: unknown

Example:
get({ obj: { address: { city: "São Paulo" } }, path: "address.city" });
// => "São Paulo"

get({ obj: { a: 1 }, path: "a.b", defaultValue: "BR" });
// => "BR"

get({ obj: { items: [{ name: "a" }, { name: "b" }] }, path: "items.1.name" });
// => "b"

Returns defaultValue (or undefined) for missing paths, null or primitive intermediates, invalid input, or empty path. Never throws.

---

### isEmpty

Check whether a given value is considered empty. Supports strings, arrays, plain objects, Maps, and Sets.

Import: import { isEmpty } from "1o1-utils/is-empty";

Signature:
function isEmpty({ value }: IsEmptyParams): boolean

Parameters:
- value (unknown, required): The value to check

Returns: boolean

What counts as empty: null, undefined, "" (empty string), [] (empty array), {} (empty plain object), empty Map, empty Set.
What is NOT empty: 0, false, class instances, non-plain objects.

Example:
isEmpty({ value: "" });        // => true
isEmpty({ value: [] });        // => true
isEmpty({ value: {} });        // => true
isEmpty({ value: null });      // => true
isEmpty({ value: new Map() }); // => true
isEmpty({ value: "hello" });   // => false
isEmpty({ value: [1, 2] });    // => false
isEmpty({ value: 0 });         // => false
isEmpty({ value: false });     // => false

---

### omit

Create a new object with the specified keys removed. Supports dot notation for nested keys.

Import: import { omit } from "1o1-utils/omit";

Signature:
function omit<T extends Record<string, unknown>>({ obj, keys }: OmitParams<T>): Record<string, unknown>

Parameters:
- obj (T, required): The source object
- keys ((keyof T | string)[], required): Keys to exclude (supports dot notation)

Returns: Record<string, unknown>

Example:
omit({ obj: { a: 1, b: 2, c: 3 }, keys: ["b", "c"] });
// => { a: 1 }

omit({ obj: { a: 1, b: { x: 10, y: 20 } }, keys: ["b.x"] });
// => { a: 1, b: { y: 20 } }

Throws: Error if obj is not an object. Error if keys is not an array.
Does not mutate the original object.

---

### pick

Create a new object containing only the specified keys. Supports dot notation for nested keys.

Import: import { pick } from "1o1-utils/pick";

Signature:
function pick<T extends Record<string, unknown>>({ obj, keys }: PickParams<T>): Record<string, unknown>

Parameters:
- obj (T, required): The source object
- keys ((keyof T | string)[], required): Keys to include (supports dot notation)

Returns: Record<string, unknown>

Example:
pick({ obj: { a: 1, b: 2, c: 3 }, keys: ["a", "c"] });
// => { a: 1, c: 3 }

pick({ obj: { a: 1, b: { x: 10, y: 20 } }, keys: ["a", "b.x"] });
// => { a: 1, b: { x: 10 } }

Throws: Error if obj is not an object. Error if keys is not an array.
Missing keys are silently skipped.

---

### set

Set a nested value on an object using a dot-notation path. Non-mutating: returns a new object with only the nodes on the path cloned (structural sharing).

Import: import { set } from "1o1-utils/set";

Signature:
function set<T extends Record<string, unknown>>({ obj, path, value, objectify }: SetParams<T>): Record<string, unknown>

Parameters:
- obj (T, required): The source object
- path (string, required): Dot-notation path (e.g. "address.city")
- value (unknown, required): The value to write at path
- objectify (boolean, optional): When true, numeric segments create plain objects with string keys instead of arrays (default: false)

Returns: Record<string, unknown>

Example:
set({ obj: { a: { b: 1 } }, path: "a.b", value: 2 });
// => { a: { b: 2 } }

set({ obj: {}, path: "items.0.name", value: "x" });
// => { items: [{ name: "x" }] }

set({ obj: {}, path: "items.0.name", value: "x", objectify: true });
// => { items: { "0": { name: "x" } } }

Throws: Error if obj is not an object. Error if path is not a string. Error if path is empty.
Does not mutate the input. Creates missing intermediate containers; arrays for numeric segments by default.

---

## Strings

### capitalize

Capitalize the first character of a string, with an option to preserve or lowercase the rest.

Import: import { capitalize } from "1o1-utils/capitalize";

Signature:
function capitalize({ str, preserveRest }: CapitalizeParams): string

Parameters:
- str (string, required): The string to capitalize
- preserveRest (boolean, optional): If true, keeps the rest as-is; if false, lowercases it (default: false)

Returns: string

Example:
capitalize({ str: "hello" });                    // => "Hello"
capitalize({ str: "hELLO", preserveRest: true }); // => "HELLO"
capitalize({ str: "hELLO" });                     // => "Hello"

Throws: Error if str is not a string.
Returns "" for empty string.

---

### slugify

Convert a string to a URL-friendly slug by lowercasing, stripping accents, and replacing non-alphanumeric characters with hyphens.

Import: import { slugify } from "1o1-utils/slugify";

Signature:
function slugify({ str }: SlugifyParams): string

Parameters:
- str (string, required): The string to slugify

Returns: string

Example:
slugify({ str: "Hello World!" });             // => "hello-world"
slugify({ str: "Cafe au lait" });             // => "cafe-au-lait"
slugify({ str: "  --Multiple   Spaces--  " }); // => "multiple-spaces"

Throws: Error if str is not a string.
Handles Unicode normalization (NFD) to strip accents. Removes leading/trailing hyphens. Collapses multiple non-alphanumeric characters into a single hyphen.

---

### transformCase

Convert a string between common case styles: camelCase, kebab-case, snake_case, PascalCase, and Title Case.

Import: import { transformCase } from "1o1-utils/transform-case";

Signature:
function transformCase({ str, to, preserveAcronyms }: TransformCaseParams): string

Parameters:
- str (string, required): The string to convert
- to ("camel" | "kebab" | "snake" | "pascal" | "title", required): Target case style
- preserveAcronyms (boolean, optional, default false): When true, all-uppercase words (length > 1) such as "HTML" are preserved instead of lowercased. In "camel", the first word is always lowercased regardless.

Returns: string

Example:
transformCase({ str: "hello world", to: "camel" });  // => "helloWorld"
transformCase({ str: "hello world", to: "kebab" });  // => "hello-world"
transformCase({ str: "hello world", to: "snake" });  // => "hello_world"
transformCase({ str: "hello world", to: "pascal" }); // => "HelloWorld"
transformCase({ str: "hello world", to: "title" });  // => "Hello World"
transformCase({ str: "myVariableName", to: "kebab" }); // => "my-variable-name"
transformCase({ str: "HTMLParser", to: "title", preserveAcronyms: true }); // => "HTML Parser"
transformCase({ str: "HTMLParser", to: "camel", preserveAcronyms: true }); // => "htmlParser"

Throws: Error if str is not a string. Error if to is not one of: camel, kebab, snake, pascal, title.
Splits on word boundaries (uppercase transitions, hyphens, underscores, spaces).

---

### truncate

Truncate a string to a specified length, appending a configurable suffix.

Import: import { truncate } from "1o1-utils/truncate";

Signature:
function truncate({ str, length, suffix }: TruncateParams): string

Parameters:
- str (string, required): The string to truncate
- length (number, required): Maximum length (positive integer)
- suffix (string, optional): String appended when truncated (default: "...")

Returns: string

Example:
truncate({ str: "Hello, World!", length: 5 });              // => "Hello..."
truncate({ str: "Hello, World!", length: 5, suffix: "..." }); // => "Hello..."
truncate({ str: "Hi", length: 10 });                         // => "Hi"

Throws: Error if str is not a string. Error if length is not a positive integer.
Returns the original string if its length does not exceed length. The suffix is appended beyond the length limit.

---

## Async

### debounce

Create a debounced version of a function that delays invocation until after a specified number of milliseconds have elapsed since the last call.

Import: import { debounce } from "1o1-utils/debounce";

Signature:
function debounce<T>({ fn, ms }: DebounceParams<T>): Debounced<T>

Parameters:
- fn (T, required): The function to debounce
- ms (number, required): Delay in milliseconds (non-negative)

Returns: Debounced<T> — The debounced function with a .cancel() method.

Example:
const debouncedSearch = debounce({
  fn: (query: string) => search(query),
  ms: 300,
});
input.addEventListener("input", (e) => debouncedSearch(e.target.value));
debouncedSearch.cancel(); // Cancel pending execution

Throws: Error if fn is not a function. Error if ms is not a number, is NaN, or is negative.
Each call resets the delay timer. .cancel() clears any pending timeout.

---

### retry

Retry an async (or sync) function up to a specified number of attempts, with configurable delay and backoff strategy.

Import: import { retry } from "1o1-utils/retry";

Signature:
async function retry<T>({ fn, attempts, delay, backoff, onRetry }: RetryParams<T>): Promise<T>

Parameters:
- fn (() => Promise<T> | T, required): The function to retry
- attempts (number, optional): Maximum attempts (positive integer, default: 3)
- delay (number, optional): Delay between retries in ms (default: 1000)
- backoff ("fixed" | "exponential", optional): Backoff strategy (default: "fixed")
- onRetry ((error, attempt) => void, optional): Callback invoked on each retry

Returns: Promise<T> — The result of the first successful call.

Example:
const data = await retry({
  fn: () => fetch("/api/data").then((r) => r.json()),
  attempts: 5,
  delay: 2000,
  backoff: "exponential",
});

await retry({
  fn: fetchData,
  onRetry: (error, attempt) => console.log(`Attempt ${attempt} failed:`, error),
});

Throws: Error if fn is not a function. Error if attempts is not a positive integer. Error if delay is negative or NaN. Error if backoff is not "fixed" or "exponential".
With exponential backoff, delay is delay * 2^i for attempt i. Throws the last error after all attempts are exhausted.

---

### safely

Wrap a function so it returns a [error, result] tuple instead of throwing. Auto-detects sync and async functions: sync calls return a tuple, async calls return a Promise of a tuple. Anticipates the TC39 Safe Assignment Operator proposal.

Import: import { safely } from "1o1-utils/safely";

Signature:
function safely<A extends unknown[], T>(fn: (...args: A) => Promise<T>): (...args: A) => Promise<[undefined, T] | [unknown, undefined]>
function safely<A extends unknown[], T>(fn: (...args: A) => T): (...args: A) => [undefined, T] | [unknown, undefined]

Parameters:
- fn ((...args: A) => T | Promise<T>, required): The function to wrap

Returns: A wrapped function. On success returns [undefined, value]. On error returns [error, undefined]. Async functions return a Promise of the tuple.

Example:
const [err, data] = safely(JSON.parse)('{"a":1}');
if (err) console.error(err); else console.log(data);

const [fetchErr, user] = await safely(fetchUser)(userId);

const safeParse = safely(JSON.parse);
const [e1, a] = safeParse('{"x":1}');
const [e2, b] = safeParse("{bad");

Throws: Error if fn is not a function. Errors caught from fn are typed as unknown — non-Error throws (strings, null, etc.) are preserved as-is. Detects async by checking the return value for a then method, so real Promises and custom thenables both work.

---

### sleep

Pause execution for a given number of milliseconds by returning a promise.

Import: import { sleep } from "1o1-utils/sleep";

Signature:
function sleep({ ms }: SleepParams): Promise<void>

Parameters:
- ms (number, required): Duration in milliseconds (non-negative)

Returns: Promise<void>

Example:
await sleep({ ms: 1000 }); // waits 1 second

Throws: Error if ms is not a number or is NaN. Error if ms is negative.
sleep({ ms: 0 }) resolves on the next tick.

---

### throttle

Create a throttled version of a function that only invokes the original at most once per specified interval.

Import: import { throttle } from "1o1-utils/throttle";

Signature:
function throttle<T>({ fn, ms }: ThrottleParams<T>): Throttled<T>

Parameters:
- fn (T, required): The function to throttle
- ms (number, required): Minimum interval between calls in milliseconds (non-negative)

Returns: Throttled<T> — The throttled function with a .cancel() method.

Example:
const throttledScroll = throttle({
  fn: () => updatePosition(),
  ms: 100,
});
window.addEventListener("scroll", throttledScroll);
throttledScroll.cancel(); // Cancel pending trailing call

Throws: Error if fn is not a function. Error if ms is not a number, is NaN, or is negative.
First call executes immediately. Subsequent calls within ms are queued; the last one fires after the interval. .cancel() clears pending calls and resets state.

---

### withTimeout

Wrap a promise with a timeout. Rejects with a TimeoutError if the promise does not settle within the given duration. Accepts an optional AbortSignal to cancel externally.

Import: import { withTimeout, TimeoutError } from "1o1-utils/with-timeout";

Signature:
function withTimeout<T>({ promise, ms, message, signal }: WithTimeoutParams<T>): Promise<T>

Parameters:
- promise (Promise<T>, required): The promise to race against the timeout
- ms (number, required): Timeout duration in milliseconds (non-negative)
- message (string | (() => string), optional): Error message or lazy factory. Default: "Operation timed out after {ms}ms"
- signal (AbortSignal, optional): Rejects with signal.reason when aborted

Returns: Promise<T> — resolves with the original value, or rejects with TimeoutError on timeout

Example:
await withTimeout({ promise: fetchData(), ms: 5000 });
// resolves normally if < 5s, throws TimeoutError otherwise

await withTimeout({ promise: slowApi(), ms: 3000, message: "API too slow" });

const controller = new AbortController();
await withTimeout({ promise: work(), ms: 10_000, signal: controller.signal });
controller.abort(); // rejects immediately with signal.reason

Throws: Error if promise is not thenable. Error if ms is not a non-negative number. Error if signal is not an AbortSignal. TimeoutError (name: "TimeoutError") if ms elapses before the promise settles. The forwarded rejection if the promise rejects before the timeout. TimeoutError is exported only from the "1o1-utils/with-timeout" subpath.

---

## Comparisons

### shallowEqual

Compare two values by their top-level entries using `Object.is`. Returns `true` when both values are strictly the same, or when they are arrays or plain objects with identical own-enumerable entries at the first level. Nested values are compared by reference, not structurally — use `deepEqual` for recursive comparison.

Import: import { shallowEqual } from "1o1-utils/shallow-equal";

Signature:
function shallowEqual({ a, b }: ShallowEqualParams): boolean

Parameters:
- a (unknown, required): The first value to compare
- b (unknown, required): The second value to compare

Returns: boolean — `true` if the values are shallowly equal, `false` otherwise.

Example:
shallowEqual({ a: { x: 1, y: 2 }, b: { x: 1, y: 2 } });
// => true

shallowEqual({ a: { x: { n: 1 } }, b: { x: { n: 1 } } });
// => false (nested refs differ)

shallowEqual({ a: [1, 2, 3], b: [1, 2, 3] });
// => true

shallowEqual({ a: Number.NaN, b: Number.NaN });
// => true (Object.is semantics)

Uses Object.is for primitive comparison, so NaN equals NaN and +0 is distinct from -0. Symbol-keyed properties, non-enumerable properties, and prototype differences are ignored. Maps, Sets, Dates, RegExps and other built-ins fall back to reference equality.

---

## Functions

### once

Create a version of a function that runs only once. Subsequent calls return the cached result from the first invocation and ignore any new arguments.

Import: import { once } from "1o1-utils/once";

Signature:
function once<T extends (...args: never[]) => unknown>(fn: T): T

Parameters:
- fn (T, required): The function to wrap

Returns: T — A wrapped function that executes fn at most once.

Example:
const init = once(() => ({ id: Math.random() }));
const a = init();
const b = init();
a === b; // true — same cached object

const getClient = once(() => new ApiClient({ token: process.env.TOKEN }));
getClient(); // creates client
getClient(); // returns same instance

Throws: Error if fn is not a function.
Arguments from the first call are forwarded to fn; later arguments are ignored. The wrapper preserves `this` from the first invocation. A return value of undefined is still cached — fn will not run again. If fn throws on the first call, the exception propagates and subsequent calls return undefined without retrying. The reference to fn is released after the first call so any state it captures can be garbage-collected.

### pipe

Compose functions left-to-right into a single function. The first function receives the initial arguments; each subsequent function receives the previous function's return value. Aligned with the TC39 pipe operator proposal.

Import: import { pipe } from "1o1-utils/pipe";

Signature:
function pipe<A extends unknown[], B, C, ..., R>(
  f1: (...args: A) => B,
  f2: (b: B) => C,
  ...
): (...args: A) => R

Parameters:
- fns (functions, variadic): Functions to compose, executed in the order provided. Overloads typed up to 10 functions; beyond that, the return type falls back to `unknown`.

Returns: A function that applies every fn in sequence. With zero arguments, returns an identity function.

Example:
const slug = pipe(
  (x: string) => x.trim(),
  (x: string) => x.toLowerCase(),
  (x: string) => x.replace(/\s+/g, "-"),
);
slug("  Hello World  "); // "hello-world"

// First function can take multiple args
const sum = pipe(
  (a: number, b: number) => a + b,
  (n: number) => n * 2,
);
sum(2, 3); // 10

Throws: Error if any argument is not a function.
Execution is synchronous. If a function returns a Promise, the next function receives the Promise (not the awaited value). `pipe()` with no arguments returns an identity function that passes its first argument through unchanged. Only the first function receives the caller's `this` context; subsequent stages are called without `this` (differs from lodash/flow and es-toolkit/flow, which forward `this` to every stage).
